coredata.dc:title: Formation, photoluminescence and in vitro bioimaging of polyethylene glycol-derived carbon dots: The molecular weight effects
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Yuxiang
Ji, Chunyu
Wu, Jiajia
Han, Qiurui
Cui, Chen
Shi, Wenquan
Peng, Zhili
coredata.dc:description: Despite their wide applications, less effort has been devoted for the elucidation of synthesis and photoluminescence mechanism of carbon dots (C-dots), which significantly limits their further developments. In this study, a series of C-dots were prepared from polyethylene glycol (PEG) of different molecular weights (M.W.), and the effects of PEG M.W. on the formation, structural and optical properties of C-dots were carefully investigated. Our study shown that the synthesis of C-dots from PEG went through an “oxidized decomposition – crosslinking – carbonization” process, and as such, the M.W. had a significant influence on the particle sizes, fluorescence intensities, surface chemistry, thermal stability, and bioimaging capabilities of C-dots. These C-dots demonstrated extremely low cytotoxicity and were good agents for cellular bioimaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Polyethylene glycol
PEG
Photoluminescence
Carbonization
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell viability assessment in the presence of C-dots
Effects of PEG M.W. on C-dots synthesis and properties

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence intensities of C-dots derived from PEG400, PEG800, PEG1500 and PEG4000 under different reaction conditions as indicated.
Fluorescence intensities of (a) PEG400, (b) PEG800, (c) PEG1500 and (d) PEG4000 under different reaction conditions as indicated.
Fluorescence intensities of C-dots synthesized from different reaction conditions: reactions under (a) 145 °C, (b) 170 °C, and (c) 195 °C; reactions run for (d) 8 h, (e) 12 h, and (f) 16 h.

TEM images of (a) C-dots400, (b) C-dots800, (c) C-dots1500 and (d) C-dots4000, the upper right insets are the enlarged picture of the specific particle, while the lower insets are the corresponding particle size distribution histograms.

(a) UV–vis spectra of C-dots derived from PEGs of different M.W., inset photographs were taken under ambient light (left) and a 400 nm handheld UV lamp (right); (b) the fluorescence emission spectrum of C-dots400 of 10 μg/mL in DI water.

The FTIR spectra (a) and full-scan XPS spectra (b) of the C-dots derived from PEGs of different M.W.; C 1s and O 1s spectra of C-dots400 (c1, c2), C-dots800 (d1, d2), C-dots1500 (e1, e2) and C-dots4000 (f1, f2).
X-ray diffraction pattern of (a) C-dots and (b) raw PEGs.
Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of the C-dots synthesized from PEGs of different M.W., samples were treated at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 under 0.1 MPa atmospheric pressure.
Cell viabilities of MDA-MB-435s cells that were treated with C-dots400, C-dots800, C-dots1500 and C-dots4000 of different concentrations as indicated for 48 h.
The fluorescence images under (a) bright field, (b) 385 nm excitation, (c) 470 nm excitation of MDA-MB-435s cells incubated with C-dots derived from PEGs of different M.W. for 4 h.
Illustration of the reaction progress for preparing C-dots from PEG.

head.ce:title: Formation, photoluminescence and in vitro bioimaging of polyethylene glycol-derived carbon dots: The molecular weight effects

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials and Energy, Yunnan University, Kunming, 650091, People's Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials and Energy, Yunnan University, Kunming 650091, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Computing Center, Materials Genome Institute, Yunnan University, Kunming, 650091, People's Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Computing Center, Materials Genome Institute, Yunnan University, Kunming 650091, P. R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Materials and Energy, Yunnan University, Kunming, 650091, People's Republic of China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Despite their wide applications, less effort has been devoted for the elucidation of synthesis and photoluminescence mechanism of carbon dots (C-dots), which significantly limits their further developments. In this study, a series of C-dots were prepared from polyethylene glycol (PEG) of different molecular weights (M.W.), and the effects of PEG M.W. on the formation, structural and optical properties of C-dots were carefully investigated. Our study shown that the synthesis of C-dots from PEG went through an “oxidized decomposition – crosslinking – carbonization” process, and as such, the M.W. had a significant influence on the particle sizes, fluorescence intensities, surface chemistry, thermal stability, and bioimaging capabilities of C-dots. These C-dots demonstrated extremely low cytotoxicity and were good agents for cellular bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (C-dots) are a new type of green, zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with diameters generally less than 10 nm. Since the discovery of C-dots in 2004 [1], significant amount of efforts have been devoted in this field thanks to their unique advantages over traditional semiconductor-based quantum dots (SQD) such as superior photoluminescence (PL) property, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, easy surface functionalization, as well as rich and economic accessibility [2–4]. As such, C-dots have found broad applications in various fields such as bioimaging, drug delivery, sensing, optoelectronics, catalysis, environment protection as well as energy conversion and storage [5–13].

Currently, the numerous synthetic methods of C-dots could be divided into two types: namely “top-down” and “bottom-up”. “Top-down” refers to the breaking of bulk carbon materials into small pieces to obtain C-dots using methods such as arc discharge, laser ablation, electrochemical peeling and oxidative acid treatment. These methods generally require relatively expensive equipment and usually with low yields. In “bottom-up” approach, hydrothermal/solvothermal reactions, microwave irradiations and ultrasonic treatments are utilized to fabricate C-dots via a “condensation-crosslinking-carbonization” process [14,15]. Compared to the “top-down” synthesis, “bottom-up” approaches generally have higher synthesis yields; form more functional groups on the surface; and produce C-dots with tunable PL. Similar to their preparation methods, precursors for C-dots synthesis are also very rich and versatile, ranging from inorganic carbon materials (i.e., graphene, carbon powders), small organic molecules (i.e., citric acid, urea) and polymers (i.e., polythiophene, polyethylene glycol), large biomolecules (i.e., chitosan) to biomasses (i.e., hair fiber, watermelon peel, rice residue) [16–23].

Due to the availability of enormous preparation methods and countless carbon precursors, our understanding towards the exact structures and PL mechanism of C-dots still remains an open debate, which significantly limit their further development and applications. In this context, there have been more studies focusing on the synthesis mechanism, structure exploration and PL elucidation of C-dots recently [24–27]. Among these studies, C-dots derived from some small organic molecules (i.e., citric acid and phenylenediamine) have been heavily studied. Citric acid (CA) is a significant type of precursor for C-dots preparation due to the ease in synthesis and the high PL quantum yields (QYs) of the C-dots formed [28–31]. As a result, many groups have reported related PL and synthetic mechanism of CA-derived C-dots. For instance, Qu et al. systematically summarized the formation and PL mechanism of C-dots through a “bottom-up” method [32]. Song et al. synthesized C-dots with CA and ethylenediamine (EDA) by hydrothermal treatment and proposed that the C-dots had two PL centers, namely the fluorescent molecule IPCA and the carbon core [15]. In addition to CA-derived C-dots, C-dots derived from phenylenediamine (PD) also have extremely high QYs and demonstrate near-infrared (NIR) PL property, which is greatly desired for bioimaging and optoelectronics. Similarly, the synthesis and PL mechanism of PD-derived C-dots have also been heavily investigated [33–35]. For instance, Liu et al. reported C-dots prepared from o-phenylenediamine (oPD) via a one-step hydrothermal treatment and ascribed the strong red PL to the efficient conjugated aromatic π systems and hydrogen bonds of the C-dots [36]. In a different study, Zhang et al. illustrated solvent-dependent PL of oPD C-dots, which was attributed to the hydrogen-bond-dominated molecular states emission [37].

In addition to small-organic-molecule carbon precursors, polymer-based carbon precursors have also been heavily investigated recently. Polymers generally have clear, repeatable and controllable structural features, thus they are very suitable for C-dots synthesis, mechanism investigation and PL tuning. Polythiophene is among the most studied polymers for C-dots synthesis [38–44], for instance, Huang et al. reported the PL tuning of NIR C-dots synthesized from polythiophene derivatives by adjusting the length of alkyl side chains [43]. Furthermore, Ge and co-workers used polythiophene quaternary ammonium and polythiophene phenylpropionic acid as precursors for the synthesis of broad absorption C-dots with high photostability and photothermal conversion efficiency [44]. Another type of polymers that have been heavily investigated are polyethylene glycol (PEG) [45–47], the simple, repeated and controllable structural features of PEG are extremely suitable for C-dots synthesis and PL mechanism exploration. For instance, Dong et al. carefully evaluated the effect of reaction temperatures on the PL properties of as-prepared C-dots from PEG [48]. Chen et al. used PEG400 to prepare C-dots with tunable emissions and systematically probed the effect of reaction time on the formation of C-dots [49]. While Zhao et al. synthesized C-dots from PEG400 in different atmospheres (pure oxygen, air and nitrogen), and elucidated the effects and reason of different atmospheres on the PL properties of C-dots formed [50].

Studies above have elucidated the formation mechanisms of C-dots from PEG and explored the effects of parameters such as reaction temperature, time, and atmosphere on the formation and properties of C-dots. Since PEG-derived C-dots need to go through an “oxidized decomposition – crosslinking – carbonization” process [49], the degree of difficulties for the oxidized decomposition of PEG with different molecular weights (M.W.) would have a heavy influence on the formation and properties of PEG-derived C-dots. However, even though PEGs are presented with various M.W., this important effect has rarely been studied in literature [51]. Herein, to the best of our knowledge, we report the first study in which the effects of PEG M.W. on the formation and chemical, structural and optical properties of C-dots were systematically investigated. Specifically, series of C-dots were synthesized from PEG of different M.W. (400, 800, 1500 and 4000) by a one-step hydrothermal method. Systematic characterizations and comparisons shown that PEG M.W. had significant influences on the particle sizes, optical properties, element ratios and thermal stability of the C-dots formed. Furthermore, mechanism studies demonstrated that the PEG C-dots formation mainly went through an “oxidized decomposition – crosslinking – carbonization” process, which could be used to guide the tuning of PEG C-dots properties. These C-dots demonstrated extremely low cytotoxicity and were good agents for cellular bioimaging.

PEGs of different M.W. (PEG400, PEG800, PEG1500 and PEG4000) were purchased from Energy Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium (DMEM) was purchased from Service Bio Technology Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was purchased from life technologies (Grand Island, NY, USA). The reagents were used as received without further purification, unless otherwise noted. Deionized water used in all experiments was made from a Master Touch-S laboratory ultrapure water machine (Master Touch, Shanghai, China).

To avoid confusion, we denote C-dots synthesized from PEGs of different M.W. as follows: C-dots400 from PEG400, C-dots800 from PEG800, C-dots1500 from PEG1500 and C-dots4000 from PEG4000. In a typical experiment, PEG (0.25 g) was added with 5 mL deionized water and sonicated for 5 min. The resulted solution was then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave, and the sealed reactor was heated in an oven at a specific temperature (i.e., 145, 170, and 195 °C) for a certain time (i.e., 8, 12 and 16 h). After cooled down to room temperature, the resulted reaction was filtered through a disposable filter to remove any bulk particles. In addition, the filtrate was dialyzed in dialysis bag (PEG400, PEG800 with 500 Da; PEG1500 with 1000 Da; and PEG4000 with 3000 Da) to remove the unreacted substance. The resulted golden yellow filtrate containing C-dots was then freeze-dried to result the oily liquid (i.e., C-dots400, C-dots800) and grey powders (i.e., C-dots1500, C-dots4000).

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were measured using an UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu, Japan), where the wavelength range was set at 195–1100 nm, the scanning speed as medium, the sampling interval as 1 nm, and the sampling was repeated twice. C-dots solutions for measurements were 10 μg/mL in DI water.

The fluorescence spectra were measured using fluorescence spectrometer (F97 Pro, Shanghai Prism Technology Co., Ltd., China). The scanning mode was set as three-dimensional wavelength scanning. The excitation and emission wavelengths were both set from 200 to 900 nm, with the width as 10 nm. The scanning speed was 1000 nm/min, and the scanning interval was 1 nm; the gain was set as 650 V. The C-dots solutions for measurements were 10 μg/mL in DI water.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured using a FTIR spectrometer (Nicoletis10, Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). First, 200 mg of potassium bromide was put into an agate mortar and fully dried at 150 °C for 30 min, then 1.5 mg of C-dots was added and grinded thoroughly. The resulted mixture was further dried at 100 °C for 5 min, and then grinded for 30 s. The grounded powder was put into a mold and pressed with a 10 t hydraulic press to form an even, smooth tablet. The tablet was then used to measure the infrared absorption spectrum with the FTIR spectrometer.

The XPS spectra were obtained on an X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (K-Alpha+, Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA), the X-ray source is monochromatic Al Kα with a hυ = 1486.6 eV photo energy operated at 6 mA × 12 kV. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was obtained on an X-ray diffractometer (DX-2700BH, Haoyuan Instrument Co., Ltd., Dandong, Liaoning, China) with a wavelength (λ) of 0.15406 nm. The particle sizes of C-dots were measured on a transmission electron microscopy (JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan) and the acceleration voltage is 200 kV. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was tested using synchronous thermal analyzer (TGA/DSC/1600LF Supreme type, Mettler Toledo, Columbus, OH, USA), where the heating rate was set as 10 °C min−1 under 0.1 MPa atmospheric pressure.
The human breast cancer cell MDA-MB-435s was grown in DMEM medium, containing 10% FBS and 1% penicillin–streptomycin (Life Technologies) at 37 °C in 5% CO2 humidified atmosphere in CO2 incubator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell viability assessment in the presence of C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The cell viability was measured using MDA-MB-435s cells upon the uptake of C-dots by 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay. Briefly, 90 μL MDA-MB-435s cells were seeded in a 96-well plate at a density of 7 × 103 cells per well and incubated at 37 °C and 5% CO2 for 24 h to ensure all the cells attached to the wells. Then, 10 μL different concentrations of C-dots were added into each well. After incubated for 48 h, 20 μL MTT solution (5 μg/mL) was added into each well and further incubated for 4 h. After that, the supernatant of each well was removed and 100 μL of DMSO was added into each well. The plant was shaken for 15 s before testing. Finally, the optical absorbance at 570 and 630 nm was measured by a microplate reader (FC, Thermo, Waltham, MA, USA) to calculate the cell viability.

For preparation, MDA-MB-435s cells were seeded in a 24-well containing 900 μL DMEM at a density of 105 cells per well and incubated for 24 h. Each C-dots (100 μL; 1 mg/mL) solution was added and further incubated for 4 h. The cells were then washed three times by normal saline and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde solution for 15 min, followed by another three times washing with normal saline. The fluorescence images were obtained on an inverted fluorescence microscope (Ni–U, Nikon, Japan).

Previous studies have demonstrated that C-dots derived from PEG need to go through an “oxidized decomposition – crosslinking – carbonization” process and the first step requires considerable energy to start, which heavily relies on the reaction parameters (i.e., reaction temperature and time) and atmospheres [48–50]. Generally speaking, with the increase of M.W., the bond breaking of polymers become much more difficult, and thus the M.W. of PEG would have certain effects on the formation of C-dots and their PL. To probe these effects, we selected four PEGs with M.W. of 400, 800, 1500 and 4000, which are denoted as PEG400, PEG800, PEG1500, and PEG4000, respectively, and compared the PL of C-dots formed. We first used a microwave reactor to synthesize C-dots, since in a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method, the reaction parameters (i.e., temperature, time, etc.) could be accurately controlled. Unfortunately, even though we tried various conditions, the resulted solution still stayed transparent and colorless with no fluorescence emission (data not shown), and the UV–vis absorption spectra of the reaction mixture and raw PEG400 are identical (Fig. S1), indicating no C-dots were formed via this microwave-assisted hydrothermal treatment.

As a result, we shifted our efforts to synthesize C-dots with hydrothermal treatment, which are known to provide a high temperature and pressure environment for reactions. To our delight, we were able to successfully prepare C-dots from PEG via hydrothermal treatment (see below). With careful experiments, we further optimized the mass of PEG and volume of water for each hydrothermal reaction and established that when 250 mg of PEG was dissolved in 5 mL of deionized water for hydrothermal treatment, the reaction would be optimal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of PEG M.W. on C-dots synthesis and properties
ce:section.ce:para: With the establishment of standard reaction conditions, we next moved to the exploration of the effects of PEG M.W. on the C-dots synthesis and their properties. As such, PEGs of different M.W., namely PEG400, PEG800, PEG1500, and PEG4000, were treated by hydrothermal method under different temperatures (145, 170 and 195 °C) with varied time durations (8, 12 and 16 h), then the respective PL of the C-dots formed were characterized and compared to explore effects of PEG M.W. on this reaction (Fig. 1 ). As can be seen, the M.W. of PEG indeed had heavy influences on the formation and properties of C-dots. Even under the exact same reaction conditions, the PEGs behaved quite differently. For instance, the reactions of PEG400 and PEG4000 under 145 °C for 16 h were quite different. Similarly, when PEG400 and PEG1500 were treated under 170 °C for 16 h, their reactions also significantly differed. However, it's worth to mention, even though M.W. of PEG had heavy influences on the formation of C-dots and their fluorescence intensities; once formed, the emission wavelengths of all the C-dots were quite similar (Fig. S2). This phenomenon could be attributed to the reason that, to form C-dots, all PEGs need to go through the same “oxidized decomposition – crosslinking – carbonization” process as discussed previously regardless of their M.W.. As such, to facilely probe the effects of PEG M.W. on C-dots formation, the fluorescence intensities of C-dots formed from PEGs of different M.W. under various conditions were characterized and compared (Fig. 1).

To probe the detailed mechanism, we first extracted performances of C-dots from same PEG under different reaciton conditions (Fig. 2 ). As can be seen, under 145 and 170 °C, the fluorescence intensities of C-dots prepared from PEG400 (C-dots400) increased first when time was extended from 8 to 12 h, and then decreased slightly as the reaction time was further extended to 16 h (Fig. 2a, black line). On the other hand, under 195 °C, the fluorescence intensity of C-dots400 had the optimum intensity at 8 h, and declined rapidly as the reaction prolonged to 16 h (Fig. 2a, blue line). It has been reported that the fluorescence of C-dots prepared from bottow-up approach was mainly decided by the core state (carbonanized core induced PL) and the molecular state (incorporated or attached fluorescent molecular fragments induced PL). At the initial stage, the molecular state (with high fluorescence QY) dominated the fluorescence; while in the later stage, where molecular fragments were gradually carbonized to form the carbon core under high temperature and prolonged reaction, the core state (with low fluorescence QY) dominated the fluorescence [14]. Thus, observations of C-dots400 fluorescence intensites could be explained as following: under 145 and 170 °C from 8 to 12 h, the molecular stage dominated the fluorescence of C-dots400. However, as the reactions were prolonged to 16 h, large quanties of fluorescent molecular fragments were carbonized to form the carbon core, and the fluorescence of C-dots400 was dominated by the core state, leading to weaker intensities. As for reactions under 195 °C, excessive carbonization was induced with short time due to the high temperature, that's why the fluorescence intensities dropped as reactions were prolonged.

Similar trends were observed for C-dots800 and C-dots1500 prepared from PEG800, and PEG1500, respectively (Fig. 2b and c). Compared to C-dots400, the slight difference is that at 145 °C, neither C-dots800 nor C-dots1500 were dominated by core states even after prolonged reaction (i.e., 16 h). This was probably due to the reason that PEGs of large M.W. (i.e., PEG800, PEG1500) are much more difficult to decompose than those with small M.W. (i.e., PEG400) [52], thus the carbonization step of the “oxidized decomposition – crosslinking – carbonization” sequences was deferred. This was further supported by observations of PEG4000 synthesis from PEG4000 (Fig. 2d). With increased M.W., PEG4000 was much harder to decompose under similar conditions. As a result, the reaction failed to generate any C-dots under 145 °C even after prolonged reaction (i.e., 16 h), only PEG4000 was recovered. Under lifted temperature (170 and 195 °C), 8 h was still not enough to produce C-dots4000; however, when the reactions were prolonged (i.e., 12 and 16 h), C-dots4000 began to form with bright fluorescence, in which the fluorescent molecular fragments were most likely the dominating fluorescence contributions.

Interestingly, reaction time had very distinct effects on the reactions of PEGs of different M.W. under low temperature (i.e., 145 °C), C-dots400, C-dots800 and C-dots1500 all demosntrated increased fluorscnece as the reaction was prolonged from 8 to 16 h, while no C-dots4000 were formed under identical conditions (Fig. 3 a). Under higher temperature (i.e., 170 and 195 °C), however, over carbonization and thus decline (even vanish) of fluorescence was observed for C-dots400, C-dots800 and C-dots1500 when reactions were run for extended time, while no over carbonization was obverved for C-dots4000 (Fig. 3b and c). Similarly, depending on the M.W. of PEG, temperature also had distinct effects on the individual reactions: with short reaction time (i.e., 8 h), C-dots400, C-dots800 and C-dots1500 all demosntrated increased fluorscnece as the reaction temperature was lifted from 145 to 195 °C, while no C-dots4000 were formed under identical conditions (Fig. 3d). With extended time (i.e., 12 and 16 h), however, similar trend of decline (and even vanish) of fluorescence was observed for C-dots400, C-dots800 and C-dots1500 when reactions were run under elevated temperatures, while no over carbonization was obverved for C-dots4000 (Fig. 3e and f).

With the detailed screening as discussed above, the optimum reaction conditions for C-dots synthesis from PEGs of different M.W. were established as following: C-dots400 (8 h, 195 °C), C-dots800 (16 h, 145 °C), C-dots1500 (8 h, 195 °C) and C-dots4000 (16 h, 195 °C). To carefully analyze and compare the morphological, optical, chemical and structural properties of these four optimized C-dots, characterizations of them by TEM microscopy, UV–vis and fluorescence, FTIR and XPS, XRD as well as TGA and zeta potential were performed. TEM microscopy was first applied to explore the morphologies and structural characteristics of these C-dots. As can be seen (Fig. 4 ), except C-dots4000 demosntrated some degree of aggregation, all the other three C-dots were well dispersed spherical particles with no obvious agglomerates. The observations corresponded well with the measurements of the surface charges of the samples via zeta potential test, which were determined to be −9.15, −20.10, −7.94 and −1.10 for C-dots400, C-dots800, C-dots1500 and C-dots4000, respectively (Fig. S3). There are noticeable lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. 4b and c, upper right insets), which coincides with the (100 fact) of graphite carbon, and the lattice spacing of 0.35 nm (Fig. 4d, upper right inset) is similar to that of graphene layers (002 fact), indicating that they have a certain degree of graphitic cores [53]. According to the particle size statistics, these particles range from 2.0 to 6.0 nm in diameters with an average diameter of 2.5, 3.5, 3.9 and 4.0 nm for C-dots400, C-dots800, C-dots1500 and C-dots4000, respectively (Fig. 4, lower insets). As can be seen, C-dots from PEG of larger M.W. have relatively larger particle size, which might be attributed to: 1) C-dots from PEG of larger M.W. had larger carbonized cores under elevated temperature and prolonged time; 2) surface passivation of C-dots from PEG of larger M.W. was achieved with larger PEG fragments.

Next, we studied the spectroscopic behaviors of the obtained samples, all the C-dots were transparent golden yellow liquid under ambient light, and turned to turquoise like color when excited by a 400 nm handheld UV lamp (Fig. 5 a, inset). Compared with carbon precursors (PEG), the UV–vis absorption spectra of C-dots have apparent peaks at 264 nm corresponding to the π-π* transitions of CC; while the peaks above 300 nm could be attributed to core absorption of C-dots (Fig. 5a) [54]. Then we studied the PL behavior of these C-dots; all the C-dots displayed the typical excitation-wavelength-dependent emission behavior (Fig. 5b, Fig. S2 b-d). As the excitation wavelengths gradually increased from 260 to 500 nm, the emission peaks shifted from 430 to 560 nm. In short, the studies above shown that although the C-dots were derived from PEGs of different M.W., they demonstrated very similar optical properties.

Following the optical properties, we then compared the chemical compositions of the C-dots by FTIR and XPS. As shown in the FTIR spectra, all the samples had very similar characteristic peaks from 1000 to 4000 cm−1 (Fig. 6 a), and these peaks resembled their carbon precursors to a great extent (Fig. S4). Specifically, peaks at 3426, 2884 and 1118 cm−1 were assigned to the O–H, C–H and C–O stretching vibrations, respectively, which were very similar to the peaks of the carbon precursor PEGs (peaks were at 3444, 2890 and 1107 cm−1, respectively, see Fig. S4). The peaks at 1652 cm−1 of the C-dots (Fig. 6a) could be attributed to the stretching vibrations of CC that originated from the carbonized core and the peaks at 1638 cm−1 of the PEGs (Fig. S4) could be assigned to the bending mode of O–H bonds of water molecules. As can be seen, compared with other C-dots and PEG precursor, C-dots400 had a distinct CO stretching vibration at 1735 cm−1, which might be due to the reason that PEG of low M.W. was more subject to oxidative dehydration to form carbonyl groups. In C-dots800, the characteristic absorption intensity ratio of CC(1652 cm−1)/C–O(1118 cm−1) reached 1.33, indicating the presence of a great amount of CC, which might be attributed to the carbonized core. On the other hand, the FTIR spectra of C-dots1500 and C-dots4000 were nearly consistent with that of PEG1500 and PEG4000, which carried the most characteristic functionalities from their carbon precursors.

We also compared the chemical compositions of the C-dots by XPS. According to the full scan survey spectra (Fig. 6b), it is clearly that all the C-dots were composed of carbon (C1s, 284 eV) and oxygen (O1s, 532 eV). Interestingly, with the M.W. of the PEG getting larger, the ratio of C/O increased, which could be attributed to that with higher M.W., PEGs are much harder subjecting to oxidative decomposition as previously discussed. Specifically, C-dots400 had a C/O ratio of 1.6, which is much lower than other three (2.1, 2.3, 2.2 for C-dots800, C-dots1500 and C-dots4000, respectively), this corresponded well with the FTIR spectra in which only C-dots400 had a distinct CO stretching vibration at 1735 cm−1.

The high-resolution C and O spectra show that the four C-dots have very similar surface functionalities. For the C-dots400, the C 1s spectra could be fitted into three binding energies: C–C/CC (284.8 eV), C–O–C/C–OH (286.5 eV) and CO (288.2 eV). The O 1s spectra could be deconvoluted into three peaks corresponding to the CO (531.5 eV), C–O–C/C–OH (532.7 eV) and –OH (534.4 eV), respectively (Fig. 6c). The C 1s and O 1s spectra of C-dots800, C-dots1500 and C-dots4000 also contain these C and O species, although their peak positions and contents are slightly different (Fig. 6d-f). Based on the FTIR and XPS analysis, we could see that the contents and ratios of C/O of the C-dots from PEGs of different M.W. were quite different, which could be attributed to the fact that the difficulty of oxidative decomposition of PEG increased as M.W. became larger. However, since the four C-dots were synthesized from similar precursors (PEGs) with the same structural unit and via the same method (hydrothermal treatment), thus it was not a surprise that the optical properties and surface functional groups of these C-dots were quite similar.

XRD characterization was further carried out to elucidate the structural changes during the transformations of PEGs to C-dots (Fig. 7 ). The broad, gentle slope peaks centered at 21.2° were typical for C-dots with small particle sizes, and often indicated the formation of carbonized core in C-dots synthesis (Fig. 7a). The characteristic peaks of PEG appeared at 19.1° and 23.2° for all PEGs except PEG400, whose crystallinity is very poor (Fig. 7b). As can be seen, only the typical broad peaks centered at 21.2° were observed for both C-dots400 and C-dots800, and the characteristic peaks of PEG at 19.1° and 23.2° completely vanished, indicating both PEG400 and PEG800 had high degree of oxidative decomposition and carbonization during the reaction. In C-dots1500, the characteristic peaks of PEG1500 were largely preserved while a weak, broad peak centered at 21.2° (Fig. 7a, red dotted lines) was also observed, indicating that partial carbonization of PEG1500 indeed took place. On the other hand, the XRD pattern of C-dots4000 preserved mostly the characteristic peaks of PEG4000, with the typical broad peak centered at 21.2° for C-dots was hardly seen, indicating the oxidative decomposition and carbonization was very difficult for PEG4000. Overall, the studies with XRD corresponded very well with the FTIR analysis and the reaction mechanisms discussed previously.

To compare the thermal stabilities of the C-dots, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was employed. Two obvious weight loss stages could be observed (Fig. 8 ): in the first stage (32–100 °C), only C-dots400 and C-dots800 had sharp weight losses accounting for 22.6 and 45.8%, respectively; while C-dots1500 and C-dots4000 hardly had any mass losses. We attribute the mass losses to the evaporation of adsorption moistures, and the dramatic differences were due to the physical form differences of the samples (C-dots400 and C-dots800 were gel form while C-dots1500 and C-dots4000 were fine powders). For the second stage, the weight losses occurred rapidly and the initiating and ending temperatures varied for the four samples, which were determined to be 170–348, 289–414, 326–432, 326–432 °C for C-dots400, C-dots800, C-dots1500 and C-dots4000, respectively. The significant weight reductions could be attributed to the decomposition of the organic functional groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl and alkyl groups as well as the PEG fragments on the C-dots. Clearly, for C-dots derived from PEG of higher M.W., it required higher temperature to decompose, which is in accordance with the C-dots formation mechanism discussed previously. It's worth noting, further increase of temperature (430–600 °C) did not change the residual masses, although the quantities left were quite small (0.60, 2.67, 2.26, 2.90% for C-dots400, C-dots800, C-dots1500 and C-dots4000, respectively), we attribute the residues to the charred carbon cellulose of the respective C-dots.

With the successful characterization of the optical, chemical, and structural properties of C-dots, we further compared their cytotoxicity. Standard MTT assays with MDA-MB-435s cells were performed to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the C-dots, and all the four samples demonstrated extremely low cytotoxicity (Fig. 9 ). As can be seen, the cell viability was almost quantitative for all the four C-dots when the concentrations were low (31.25 μg/mL), and even when the concentrations were increased to 500 μg/mL, the cells still maintained a high viability that was over 90% for the C-dots, demonstrating the high biocompatibility of our C-dots. Considering the high biocompatibility of PEG, this finding was not surprise at all.

Collectively consider the bright PL, low cytotoxicity and excellent cellular uptake of these C-dots; we applied them for in vitro cellular bioimaging. To our delight, all the samples were successfully picked up by the cells after 4 h of incubation (Fig. 10 ). Under 385 nm excitation light, the cells treated with C-dots maintained their normal morphology and revealed strong blue fluorescent emissions; similarly, strong green fluorescent emissions could be observed under 470 nm excitation light. A close look at the figures revealed that C-dots derived from PEG with higher M.W. (C-dots1500 and C-dots4000) had a much better cellular imaging capability than that of C-dots derived from PEG with lower M.W. (C-dots400 and C-dots800). Interestingly, for all the C-dots, the samples were mainly located in the cellular membranes and cytoplasm, which is common for most C-dots.

In summary, a series of C-dots were successfully synthesized from PEGs of different M.W.. Our study shown that the M.W. had significant effects on the formation and properties of C-dots. As such, to obtain optimum C-dots from PEGs of different M.W., reaction parameters such as temperature and time should be carefully and individually optimized. Mechanism explorations further confirmed that reactions using PEG to prepare C-dots indeed mainly went through an “oxidized decomposition – crosslinking – carbonization” process as previously reported. Careful characterization of the samples revealed that the C-dots derived from PEGs of different M.W. demonstrated similar emission wavelengths and surface functionalities, which could be attributed to their same synthesis method and the same basic structural unit of PEG. However, the particle sizes, fluorescence intensities, element ratios and thermal stability of C-dots were heavily influenced by the M.W. of the PEG precursors, which provided a convenient and reliable approach for researchers to fine tune the properties of PEG C-dots. These C-dots demonstrated extremely low cytotoxicity and were good agents for cellular bioimaging.

Yuxiang Jiang: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Chunyu Ji: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Jiajia Wu: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis. Qiurui Han: Investigation. Chen Cui: Investigation. Wenquan Shi: Formal analysis. Zhili Peng: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Z. Peng gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China under the grant number: 21807010, and the Applied Basic Research Program of Yunnan Province under the grant number: 2019FB066. Authors thank Advanced Analysis and Measurement Center of Yunnan University for the sample testing service.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Treethammathurot, B.; Ovartlarnporn, C.; Wungsintaweekul, J.; Duncan, R.; Wiwattanapatapee, R., Effect of peg molecular weight and linking chemistry on the biological activity and thermal stability of pegylated trypsin. Int. J. Pharm. 2008, 357 (1-2), 252-259.

Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.
